2020 · NeurIPS · Foundation Models

Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

An autoregressive Transformer scaled to 175 billion parameters that learns new tasks from a handful of examples placed in its prompt — no gradient updates, no task-specific fine-tuning — reframing "training a model for a task" as conditioning on a context.

Brown, Mann, Ryder, Subbiah, Kaplan, … Sutskever, Amodei · 31 authors
venue NeurIPS 2020 citations 59,482 topic Foundation Models source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2019 the field had converged on a single recipe: pre-train then fine-tune. Pre-train a Transformer on a mountain of text, then update its weights on thousands of labeled examples for each downstream task. It worked — but it carried three structural costs the paper names directly.

First, every new task needs its own large labeled dataset, which caps how broadly a model can be applied. Second, fine-tuning on a single narrow distribution lets a model exploit spurious correlations: the more expressive the model and the narrower the task, the easier it is to ace the benchmark without generalizing — so strong fine-tuned scores can overstate true task ability and degrade out-of-distribution. Third, it doesn't match how people work: a human picks up a new task from a short instruction ("tell me if this is happy or sad") or a couple of demonstrations, not 10,000 gradient steps.

GPT-2 had already hinted that a language model could do tasks zero-shot — with no examples — but at levels far below fine-tuned systems (e.g. 4% on Natural Questions, 55 F1 on CoQA, more than 35 points behind the state of the art). The question GPT-3 puts to the test is blunt: can sheer scale close that gap? If a model is big enough, can it perform a task from only a description and/or a few demonstrations sitting in its context window, with the weights frozen?

// how it works

The method

The architecture is deliberately boring. GPT-3 is the same decoder-only Transformer as GPT-2 — including its alternating dense and locally-banded sparse attention — trained with the ordinary autoregressive next-token objective. Nothing about the model is new. What is new is scale: 175 billion parameters (96 layers, d_model = 12,288, 96 attention heads), 10× larger than any previous non-sparse language model. It is trained on roughly 300 billion tokens sampled from filtered Common Crawl (45 TB of plaintext cut to 570 GB ≈ 400 billion byte-pair tokens), plus WebText2, two book corpora, and Wikipedia. To trace how behavior changes with size, they train eight models from 125M to 175B parameters under matched settings.

𝓛(θ) = − Σi log Pθ(xi | x1 … xi−1) // the entire training signal — plain autoregressive language modeling, no task labels

The conceptual move is to treat the forward pass itself as the learning algorithm. The paper frames a single ability — "in-context learning" — along a spectrum of how much help the prompt gives:

  • Zero-shot — a natural-language task description and the query, nothing else.
  • One-shot — the description plus a single worked demonstration.
  • Few-shot — the description plus K demonstrations (typically 10–100), all packed into the context window.

In every case the weights never change. There is an outer loop — stochastic gradient descent over pre-training — that instills a general capacity, and an inner loop — conditioning on the examples in the prompt at inference time — that adapts to a specific task. It is meta-learning without any explicit meta-training objective: "few-shot learning" is just the conditional probability below, evaluated with θ frozen.

P(answer | query) ≈ Pθ( y | (x1,y1) … (xK,yK), xquery ) // the K demonstrations live in the context — θ is never updated
// what they found

The results

The load-bearing finding is not any single benchmark — it is a shape. In-context performance rises smoothly and steeply with model size, and the advantage of few-shot over zero-shot widens as models get larger: bigger models are not just better, they are better at using the examples in their prompt. Aggregated across 42 accuracy-denominated benchmarks, the 175B model in the few-shot setting sits far above smaller models and well above its own zero-shot curve.

On individual tasks the few-shot 175B model is, in several cases, genuinely state of the art despite never being fine-tuned:

  • LAMBADA (long-range word prediction): 76.2% zero-shot and 86.4% few-shot, versus a prior SOTA of 68.0% — perplexity drops to 1.92.
  • TriviaQA (open-domain QA): 64.3 → 68.0 → 71.2% across zero/one/few-shot, the last surpassing the fine-tuned open-domain state of the art.
  • CoQA: 81.5 → 84.0 → 85.0 F1 — within a few points of fine-tuned systems.
  • SuperGLUE: few-shot average 71.8, surpassing a fine-tuned BERT-Large.
  • Arithmetic emerges with scale: few-shot, the 175B model hits 100% on 2-digit addition, 98.9% on 2-digit subtraction, 80.2% on 3-digit addition, 94.2% on 3-digit subtraction — while the 13B model can only do 2-digit add/subtract about half the time, a sharp capability jump near the top of the size range.
  • Generated news: humans tell ~200-word articles written by the 175B model from real ones at just ~52% accuracy (≈ the 50% chance level), versus ~86% for a deliberately low-quality control model.

The honest counterweight, which the paper reports plainly: few-shot is not yet parity with fine-tuning. On SuperGLUE the fine-tuned SOTA is 89.0 vs GPT-3's 71.8; natural-language inference (WiC, RTE-style comparison tasks) hovers near chance; and open-book reading comprehension and Natural Questions remain weak. Scale moves the whole frontier, but unevenly.

BenchmarkZero-shotOne-shotFew-shot
LAMBADA (acc)76.272.586.4
TriviaQA (acc)64.368.071.2
CoQA (F1)81.584.085.0
2-digit addition——100
// does it hold up?

The eval read

GPT-3's evaluation is sprawling and, to the authors' credit, unusually candid about its own holes — but the holes are real and worth naming precisely. The single biggest one is contamination. Training on 570 GB of 2016–2019 Common Crawl all but guarantees that benchmark test and dev sets leaked into the corpus. The paper tried to deduplicate, then discloses a bug that left filtering incomplete on long documents; retraining was too expensive, so instead they ran a post-hoc "clean vs dirty" analysis and argue the effect is small. That analysis is a reasonable mitigation, not a clean bill of health — and some sets (the Children's Book Test) showed near-total overlap and were simply dropped. Any single benchmark number should be read through that lens.

The baseline framing also needs care. GPT-3's few-shot scores are placed beside fine-tuned SOTA systems that consumed the entire labeled training set; few-shot sees ≤100 examples and no gradient updates. Where GPT-3 wins (LAMBADA, open-domain TriviaQA) that asymmetry makes the result striking; where it loses (SuperGLUE 71.8 vs 89.0, NLI near chance) the gap is large and the comparison flatters neither side cleanly. And most numbers are reported on development sets — the authors only submitted to held-out test servers for SuperGLUE, TriviaQA, and PIQA — so the headline table is not all true held-out test.

Finally, a single "few-shot accuracy" hides variance: results depend on the choice, format, and ordering of the in-context examples, and on the per-task K and prompt the authors hand-selected. The aggregate curve is the robust claim; individual cells carry error bars the table doesn't show. Bias is documented but unfixed — sentiment varies by the race named in the prompt, occupations skew gendered, religions draw stereotyped completions — the model faithfully reflects its corpus.

baselines

Few-shot (≤100 in-context examples, frozen weights) is compared against fine-tuned SOTA that used the full training set — impressive where GPT-3 wins, but an apples-to-oranges gap where it loses (SuperGLUE 71.8 vs 89.0; NLI near chance).

contamination

Web-scale Common Crawl pre-training leaked test/dev sets; a disclosed dedup bug left filtering incomplete and retraining was infeasible. A post-hoc clean-vs-dirty check argues small impact, but it is mitigation, not proof.

what's missed

The aggregate curve masks uneven skills (strong completion/QA/arithmetic, weak NLI/reading comprehension), reports mostly dev-set numbers, hides prompt-choice variance, and leaves measured social bias unaddressed.

would raise confidence

Held-out test submissions across the board, a contamination-controlled retrain, reported variance over prompt/example choice, and probes that separate memorization from reasoning on the arithmetic and QA wins.

// bottom line

GPT-3 did not beat fine-tuned SOTA everywhere, and its rigor (dev-set reporting, acknowledged contamination, hidden prompt variance) leaves room to argue with any one number. But the load-bearing result isn't a benchmark — it is the smooth, steep scaling of in-context learning, which turned "train a model for a task" into "write a prompt" and set the agenda for everything that followed.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Few-shot in-context learning. A 175B language model adapts to new tasks from a few examples in its prompt with zero gradient updates — the forward pass is the learning algorithm, and "training for a task" becomes prompting.
  2. Scale is the mechanism. In-context ability, and the few-shot-over-zero-shot gap, grow smoothly and steeply with model size across 42 benchmarks; some skills (multi-digit arithmetic) only appear near the 175B end — an early sighting of emergence.
  3. The caveats are part of the legacy. Dev-set reporting, an acknowledged train/test contamination bug, prompt-choice variance, and documented social bias are exactly the eval-rigor questions that web-scale models — and every benchmark in this atlas — still have to answer.
